attributes.stem:
A 65-year-old man presents with a two-week history of swelling of the neck and discolouration of his face. He has not had any similar symptoms before. Over the past two years, he has noticed that his exercise tolerance has decreased; he can now only walk 100 metres before becoming short of breath. He does not report any chest pain, fever, or tremors. He has a 60-pack-year smoking history and drinks 1 glass of wine a week.

On examination, his vitals are all normal; however, his jugular venous pressure is elevated and he has swelling of his hands. Furthermore, his face appears flushed (Image). His heart sounds are dual with no murmurs, lungs are clear, and abdomen is soft and not tender. He has no ankle oedema and peripheral pulses are present.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following investigations is most likely to determine the cause of his symptoms?
attributes.explanation:
This patient has a two-week history of neck swelling and facial discolouration, as well as jugular venous distension on examination. This is most consistent with a diagnosis of superior vena cava (SVC) obstruction. An extrinsic tumour compressing on the SVC is the most common cause of SVC obstruction. The facial discolouration could be exaggerated by asking the patient to lift his hands over his head (Pemberton's sign), thus narrowing the thoracic inlet, with any mass at that site worsening the obstruction. In the light of his extensive smoking history, this obstruction is most likely caused by primary lung cancer (bronchogenic carcinoma). Thus, a chest x-ray is the most likely investigation to determine the cause of his symptoms.

A thyroid ultrasound would potentially reveal a large thyroid mass; which is a rare cause of SVC obstruction. Despite this, it is a far less common cause than bronchogenic carcinoma.

Although an echocardiogram may be useful in evaluating if this patient has any cardiac component to his recent deteriorating shortness of breath, and may also be useful for imaging the SVC for thrombosis; it is not likely to show a bronchogenic carcinoma.

Although a CT abdomen is useful in determining masses that may be causing an inferior vena cava obstruction; this patient is showing symptoms of a superior vena cava obstruction; thus, a CT abdomen would be far less useful.

An endoscopy is unlikely to provide any diagnostic value in an SVC obstruction or a bronchogenic carcinoma.
learningPoints: A superior vena cava obstruction can present with swelling of the face, neck, and arms, shortness of breath, and jugular venous distention.
The most common cause of superior vena cava obstruction is a primary bronchogenic carcinoma.
references: Seligson MT, Surowiec SM. Superior Vena Cava Syndrome. [Updated 2021 Jul 13]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2021 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK441981/
